title: The Faith of the Centurion part 4
scripture_text: “When Jesus had finished saying all this the people who were listening, he entered Capernaum. There a centurion’s servant, whom his master valued highly, was sick and about to die. The centurion heard of Jesus and sent some elders of the Jews to him, asking him to come and heal his servant. When they came to Jesus, they pleaded earnestly with him, “This man deserves to have you do this, because he loves our nation and has built our synagogue.” So Jesus went with them He was not far from the house when the centurion sent friends to say to him: “Lord, don’t trouble yourself, for I do not deserve to have you come under my roof. That is why I did not even consider myself worthy to come to you. But say the word, and my servant will be healed. For I myself am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I tell this one, ‘Go,’ and he goes; and that one, ‘Come,’ and he comes. I say to my servant, ‘Do this,’ and he does it.” When Jesus heard this, he was amazed at him, and turning to the crowd following him, he said, “I tell you, I have not found such great faith even in Israel.” Then the men who had been sent, returned to the house and found the servant well.”
devotional:
C. Countercultural living requires a life that expresses radical love.

Luke tell us that ‘a highly valued slave of a Roman Centurion was sick and near death.’ This phrase is full of controversy. In Roman law, a slave was defined as a living tool. He had no rights. In fact, a master could abuse him and even kill him if he chose to do so.

A Roman writer on estate management recommended the farmer to examine his implements every year and to throw out those which were old and broken, and to do the same with his own slaves. Normally when a slave was past his ability to work, he was thrown out to die. They were bought and sold at slave markets across the ancient world, and were paraded by their owners as a sign of wealth: the more enslaved people a person owned, it was thought, the greater their stature and wealth. They were considered the property of their masters, enslaved Romans were often subjected to vile treatment, including physical and sexual abuse.

This centurion esteemed, respected, and had a high regard for this slave. He didn’t look down on him, or belittle him, or see him as less than. The respect we show or don’t show to others says more about us than it does about them.

Does respect need to be earned?

Do others need to deserve our respect? Respect cannot be demanded, nor should be tied to someone’s status or position in life. Every person who is made in God’s image has value and worth no matter their status.

D. Countercultural living will require us to have relevant relationships with the community.

Luke tells us, ‘When the officer heard about Jesus, he sent some respected Jewish elders to ask him to come and heal his slave. So they earnestly begged Jesus to help the man. “If anyone deserves your help, he does,” they said, “for he loves the Jewish people and even built a synagogue for us.’

These verses are amazing! Here we have a pagan solider enlisted in the Roman army. He is respected by the Jewish religious leaders and he built a synagogue. The incredible thing is not that he was prepared to pay for the building of the synagogue, but that the Jewish leaders let him build it in the first place. In these days the predominate world-view of Judaism was aggressively anti-gentile. The Gentiles were despised and hated by the Jews, yet somehow, this member of an occupying enemy force was able to remove all the barriers between himself and his antagonists so he could show love. Some people think that this centurion may have been a convert to Judaism, but this is unlikely as Jewish men were not allowed to enlist into the Roman army.

Can you grasp how counter-cultural this man was? He did not allow the stereotypical norms or expectations of society to dictate his actions. He loved his enemy, he did good to those who hated him. This was authentic faith, living out a set of beliefs that enabled him to touch the very heart of community.
prayer: Father help me to show the difference that Jesus can make in someone's life.
html:
The Faith of the Centurion part 4

Study Text: Luke 7:1-10
“When Jesus had finished saying all this the people who were listening, he entered Capernaum. There a centurion’s servant, whom his master valued highly, was sick and about to die. The centurion heard of Jesus and sent some elders of the Jews to him, asking him to come and heal his servant. When they came to Jesus, they pleaded earnestly with him, “This man deserves to have you do this, because he loves our nation and has built our synagogue.” So Jesus went with them He was not far from the house when the centurion sent friends to say to him: “Lord, don’t trouble yourself, for I do not deserve to have you come under my roof. That is why I did not even consider myself worthy to come to you. But say the word, and my servant will be healed. For I myself am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I tell this one, ‘Go,’ and he goes; and that one, ‘Come,’ and he comes. I say to my servant, ‘Do this,’ and he does it.” When Jesus heard this, he was amazed at him, and turning to the crowd following him, he said, “I tell you, I have not found such great faith even in Israel.” Then the men who had been sent, returned to the house and found the servant well.”

C. Countercultural living requires a life that expresses radical love.

Luke tell us that ‘a highly valued slave of a Roman Centurion was sick and near death.’ This phrase is full of controversy. In Roman law, a slave was defined as a living tool. He had no rights. In fact, a master could abuse him and even kill him if he chose to do so.

A Roman writer on estate management recommended the farmer to examine his implements every year and to throw out those which were old and broken, and to do the same with his own slaves. Normally when a slave was past his ability to work, he was thrown out to die. They were bought and sold at slave markets across the ancient world, and were paraded by their owners as a sign of wealth: the more enslaved people a person owned, it was thought, the greater their stature and wealth. They were considered the property of their masters, enslaved Romans were often subjected to vile treatment, including physical and sexual abuse.

This centurion esteemed, respected, and had a high regard for this slave. He didn’t look down on him, or belittle him, or see him as less than. The respect we show or don’t show to others says more about us than it does about them.

Does respect need to be earned?

Do others need to deserve our respect? Respect cannot be demanded, nor should be tied to someone’s status or position in life. Every person who is made in God’s image has value and worth no matter their status.

D. Countercultural living will require us to have relevant relationships with the community.

Luke tells us, ‘When the officer heard about Jesus, he sent some respected Jewish elders to ask him to come and heal his slave. So they earnestly begged Jesus to help the man. “If anyone deserves your help, he does,” they said, “for he loves the Jewish people and even built a synagogue for us.’

These verses are amazing! Here we have a pagan solider enlisted in the Roman army. He is respected by the Jewish religious leaders and he built a synagogue. The incredible thing is not that he was prepared to pay for the building of the synagogue, but that the Jewish leaders let him build it in the first place. In these days the predominate world-view of Judaism was aggressively anti-gentile. The Gentiles were despised and hated by the Jews, yet somehow, this member of an occupying enemy force was able to remove all the barriers between himself and his antagonists so he could show love. Some people think that this centurion may have been a convert to Judaism, but this is unlikely as Jewish men were not allowed to enlist into the Roman army.

Can you grasp how counter-cultural this man was? He did not allow the stereotypical norms or expectations of society to dictate his actions. He loved his enemy, he did good to those who hated him. This was authentic faith, living out a set of beliefs that enabled him to touch the very heart of community.

Prayer: Father help me to show the difference that Jesus can make in someone's life.
Bible in a year reading plan: - Judges 6-7
